epigraph.text: So the last will be first, and the first last.
epigraph.quote: The logarithm is the inverse of the exponentialâ€”but in the complex domain, inverse becomes complicated.

content.text:
Since $e^z$ is not one-to-one (it has period $2\pi i$), its inverse must 
be multi-valued. This leads us to the fascinating world of branch cuts 
and Riemann surfaces.

content.title: Complex Logarithm
content.content:
For $z \neq 0$, a **logarithm** of $z$ is 
any complex number $w$ such that $e^w = z$. We write $w = \log z$, 
understanding this to be a *multi-valued* function.

content.title: Formula for the Logarithm
content.content:
For $z \neq 0$,
\[
 \log z = \ln|z| + i\arg(z),
\]
where $\arg(z)$ denotes any argument of $z$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $w = \log z$, then $e^w = z$. Writing $w = u + iv$ and $z = re^{i\theta}$:
\[
 e^{u + iv} = e^u e^{iv} = re^{i\theta}.
\]
Thus $e^u = r$ (so $u = \ln r = \ln|z|$) and $v = \theta + 2\pi k$ for some 
integer $k$. Hence $w = \ln|z| + i(\theta + 2\pi k)$.

content.text:
Since $\arg(z)$ can differ by multiples of $2\pi$, $\log z$ has infinitely 
many values, differing by $2\pi i$.

content.title: Multi-valued logarithm
content.problem: Find all values of $\log(-1)$ and $\log i$.
content.solution:
For $\log(-1)$: $|-1| = 1$ and $\arg(-1) = \pi + 2\pi k$. Thus 
$\log(-1) = \ln 1 + i(\pi + 2\pi k) = i\pi(2k + 1)$ for $k \in \Z$.

For $\log i$: $|i| = 1$ and $\arg(i) = \pi/2 + 2\pi k$. Thus 
$\log i = i(\pi/2 + 2\pi k)$ for $k \in \Z$.

content.title: Principal Logarithm
content.content:
The **principal value of the logarithm** is
\[
 \Log z = \ln|z| + i\Arg(z),
\]
where $\Arg(z) \in (-\pi, \pi]$ is the principal argument.

content.text:
The principal logarithm $\Log$ is a single-valued function defined on 
$\C \setminus \{0\}$. However, it is *not* continuous on the negative 
real axis: as $z$ crosses from the upper half-plane to the lower half-plane, 
$\Arg(z)$ jumps from $\pi$ to $-\pi$, causing $\Log z$ to jump by $2\pi i$.

content.title: Branch Cut
content.content:
A **branch cut** is a curve removed from the domain 
to make a multi-valued function single-valued and continuous. For $\Log z$, 
the standard branch cut is the non-positive real axis $(-\infty, 0]$.

content.title: Analyticity of the Principal Logarithm
content.content:
The function $\Log z$ is analytic on $\C \setminus (-\infty, 0]$ with
\[
 \frac{d}{dz}\Log z = \frac{1}{z}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Branch of the Logarithm
content.content:
A **branch** of $\log z$ is a single-valued, continuous 
function $L(z)$ defined on a domain $D$ such that $e^{L(z)} = z$ for all 
$z \in D$.

content.text:
Different branches differ by constants $2\pi ki$. On a simply connected 
domain not containing the origin, a continuous branch of the logarithm 
can always be defined.

content.title: Bernhard Riemann (1826--1866)
content.content:
Riemann's revolutionary idea was to visualize multi-valued functions on 
**Riemann surfaces**: instead of the complex plane, we consider 
multiple "sheets" connected along branch cuts. The logarithm lives 
naturally on an infinite helical surface.

marginNotes.text:
The Lord is the everlasting God, the Creator 
of the ends of the earth. He does not faint or grow weary; his understanding 
is unsearchable.

marginNotes.author: John Napier (1614)
marginNotes.text:
Seeing there is nothing that is so 
troublesome to mathematical practice... than the multiplications, 
divisions, square and cubical extractions of great numbers... I began 
therefore to consider by what certain and ready art I might remove 
those hindrances.
